concept.headline: Functions in Rust are defined with fn, typed explicitly, and return the last expression.
concept.explanation: A function packages a reusable block of logic. In Rust, every parameter must have a type annotation and every non-void function must declare its return type after ->. The body is a block of statements and optionally a final expression. That final expression (written without a semicolon) is implicitly returned — no `return` keyword needed.
keyPoints: All parameters require explicit type annotations
Return type declared with -> after the parameter list
The last expression without ; is the implicit return value
Placing ; after the last expression makes it a statement — returns () instead
Functions with no -> implicitly return the unit type ()

demo.intro: Watch a function receive two parameters, compute a result, and return it to the caller.

explanation.why: x will be passed as the first argument to add()
explanation.intent: Prepare a value to use as an argument
explanation.misconception: Arguments are passed by value for Copy types like i32 — x is copied into the parameter a, not moved

explanation.why: y will be passed as the second argument to add()
explanation.misconception: Same as x — i32 is Copy, so y is not consumed by the function call

explanation.why: Control flow transfers to the add function; copies of x and y are bound to parameters a and b
explanation.misconception: A new scope is created for the function — a and b only exist inside add()

explanation.why: The expression a + b (no semicolon) is the last item in the function body — it is the implicit return value
explanation.intent: Compute the sum and return it without an explicit return keyword
explanation.misconception: If you wrote a + b; (with semicolon) the function would return () — the unit type — and the code would not compile because the signature says -> i32

explanation.why: The caller receives the returned value and binds it to sum with let
explanation.change: sum = 7 (new i32 slot in main's scope)
explanation.intent: Capture the function result for later use
explanation.misconception: sum is a new, independent variable — changing it does not affect anything inside add()

explanation.why: println! formats sum into the template string and outputs to stdout
explanation.intent: Verify the function returned the correct result
explanation.misconception: The string literal 'sum = ' is not a variable — it is concatenated at compile time into the format string

options: () — unit type, compile error for -> i32
prediction.explanation: The semicolon after n * 2 turns it into a statement that produces (). The function body then returns () but the signature promises i32 — this is a compile error. Remove the semicolon to fix it.

explanation.afterDemo: The function add received copies of x and y, computed a + b in its own scope, and returned 7 to main. The caller bound that 7 to sum. Notice how the semicolon rule controls what a function returns — it is a deliberate design that makes return values explicit at a glance.
keyTakeaways: The last expression without a semicolon is the implicit return.
Adding a semicolon makes it a statement — the function returns () instead.
i32 and other scalar types are Copy — they are duplicated when passed to functions.

practice.prompt: Write a function called `square` that takes an i32 and returns its square (n * n). Call it with 6 and print the result.
practice.hint: The body should be just n * n (no semicolon) as the last expression. Call: let result = square(6);

feedback.successMessage: Well done! square correctly returns n * n using an implicit return expression.
retryHints: Make sure n * n has no semicolon at the end — otherwise the function returns () and will not compile.
The return type must be i32 — match the type of n * n.
Call with square(6) and the expected output is 36.

summary.title: Functions — Complete
takeaways: fn name(params) -> ReturnType { } is the full function signature.
The final expression (no semicolon) is returned implicitly.
Scalar types like i32 are copied when passed to functions.
